next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Doug took his pills in the basement bathroom with the door locked. Unnecessary. Nate was upstairs, talking through the floorboards in that pitch he used when he wanted other people to hear how calm he was.

Lexapro first. Escitalopram oxalate, 10 milligrams, once daily with or without food. Clinical data said absorption ticked up a little with food, and Doug hadn't eaten since yesterday's lunch. Dry swallow. Chalk on the tongue. Three months now, same as the divorce. The prescription had arrived two weeks after Steph left. Maybe it was working. Maybe he was just getting used to waking up in a different life.

Ativan next. Lorazepam, 0.5 milligrams, as needed. It was needed.

For years he'd sold both classes of drug, back when brand names still mattered and lunch got you into the back office. Now he tipped one into his palm and took it from the tap with the same hand he'd once used to pass out sample cards. The mirror showed nothing new except the number on the scale back in Richfield: twenty-seven pounds gone since Steph said she was done. Best diet he'd ever been on. Hard to recommend it with a straight face.

Both bottles were in his duffel now, zipped into the side pocket under a rolled pair of socks. They'd started the weekend in the shared upstairs bathroom, tucked between Tommy's industrial bottle of Advil and Paul's toothpaste after everyone dumped their gear there on Thursday evening. Toothbrushes, razors, Cliff's moisturizer nobody was allowed to mention, the same clutter they made every year. Doug's bottles had sat there through Thursday night and all...

prev_tail_excerpt:
...ce of somebody waiting for his own body to catch up with him.

"All right," she said. "Come find me when you've got them in you."

He gave her a quick glance at that, something like embarrassment and gratitude getting mixed together and canceled out, then nodded again. "Yeah."

She walked back up to the house before he'd had to decide whether to say anything else. He didn't call her back. He didn't need to. She had what she came for.

Mallick sat at the kitchen table and turned to a fresh page in her notebook. The refrigerator kicked on with its grinding cycle. She wrote:

*Decker: knows body position. Says CPR. Scene too clean for CPR. Check bruising, sheets, clock.*

*Haywood: knows study. Two doors past half bath. Been in there recently. Debt? Papers?*

*Beckman: lawyer. Angry. Won't explain argument. Maybe clean hands, maybe careful ones.*

*Renaldi: half bath when I came in. Wet hands. Next.*

*Griggs: later today. Talks too much. Will correct himself.*

*Whitfield: dock. "Forgot meds." Wants delay, not escape. Later today.*

She looked at the list. Six men. Five of them said they went to bed, slept through the night, and woke up to the news that their friend was dead. By morning the body had been handled, the house had been handled, and at least two of the stories in front of her had already been adjusted to fit the daylight.

She tore the page out, folded it into her blazer pocket, and went to find her phone. She wanted the BCA to move on the autopsy before supper, and she wanted Renaldi at the table before the others had another hour to listen to each other breathe.
